title: Conserving relaxation-time approximation for electron-electron collisions
authors: Sayak Bhattacharjee, Tatia Kiliptari, Dmitrii L. Maslov
abstract: We develop a conserving relaxation-time approximation (cRTA), which explicitly enforces conservation of particle number, energy, and momentum and employs an energy-resolved projection onto the full space of collision invariants. This makes the cRTA applicable to transport quantities sensitive to the energy dependence of the nonequilibrium quasiparticle distribution near the Fermi surface. We benchmark the cRTA against several exact and asymptotically exact results. In particular, it reproduces the low- and high-temperature limits of the dc conductivity of a non-Galilean-invariant Fermi liquid (FL) with disorder, the hydrodynamic and collisionless limits of the finite wavevector longitudinal conductivity of a clean Galilean-invariant FL, and the asymptotic scaling forms of the optical conductivity of a clean non-Galilean-invariant FL beyond the semiclassical limit. For several observables, the agreement with exact solutions is quantitative at the percent level. These results demonstrate that the cRTA provides a simple and accurate framework for transport beyond the Fermi surface projection approximation.
summary: This paper introduces a new 'conserving' relaxation-time approximation that preserves particle number, energy, and momentum in kinetic equations. It provides a highly accurate and simple framework for calculating transport properties in Fermi liquids beyond the standard semiclassical limits.
main_problem: The standard relaxation-time approximation (RTA) fails to enforce fundamental conservation laws (particle number, energy, and momentum) and is often limited to the Fermi surface projection approximation, making it inaccurate for transport quantities sensitive to energy-dependent quasiparticle distributions.
main_result: The authors developed a conserving relaxation-time approximation (cRTA) that explicitly enforces conservation laws via an energy-resolved projection, achieving quantitative agreement (within 1-6%) with exact solutions for DC, longitudinal, and optical conductivity across various regimes.
method: The cRTA uses an algebraic construction of the collision integral and an energy-resolved projection onto a hydrodynamic space of collision invariants, utilizing Wigner transforms and Moyal products to handle frequency-dependent driving terms.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to single-band electron systems with arbitrary dispersion, including Galilean-invariant and non-Galilean-invariant Fermi liquids, such as 2D Dirac and 3D Weyl fermions, with both clean and disordered limits.
key_observables: DC conductivity, longitudinal conductivity, thermal conductivity, and optical conductivity.
important_parameters: Scattering times (ee and impurity), frequency (Omega), wavevector (q), temperature (T), and frequency-to-temperature ratio (Omega/T).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a quasiparticle approximation, linear response to electric fields, and an energy-independent electron-electron scattering rate (neglecting logarithmic corrections in 2D).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the relative deviation between cRTA and exact results for different parameters and an inset displaying the temperature-dependent part of the exact solution.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the cRTA framework, details its mathematical construction and enforcement of conservation laws, benchmarks the method against exact results in various transport regimes (hydrodynamic, collisionless, viscoelastic), and discusses its application to specific systems like Weyl fermions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Main panel: Relative deviation between the cRTA and exact results, Eqs. (16) and (20), respectively. Solid (black), dashed (blue), and dotted (red) lines correspond to b = 0, 1, 0.732 in Eq. (11), respectively. Inset: the T-dependent part of the exact solution in units of e2, for Ω2 pτi/εF = 100.
